title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who discuss their experience of working from home. For questions 43-52, choose from the people (A-D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Home Office: Four People on Remote Working

texts.A:
### Maya, 34, Software Engineer
[43]The unexpected benefit I discovered was that I could finally concentrate for extended periods without the constant interruptions of an open-plan office.[/43] I had assumed I would miss the social element, and in some ways I do, but the quality of my output has improved significantly. [48]Having more control over my schedule has also meant I can attend my daughter's school events without having to negotiate time off with a manager.[/48] I do make a point of logging off at a fixed time each day, which took discipline to establish but has transformed my wellbeing. [51]I have also started walking at lunchtime every day, something I never did when commuting, and the improvement in my energy levels has been remarkable.[/51]
texts.B:
### Leon, 48, Sales Manager
I was sceptical at first about whether my team could function without being in the same building. [44]I now send individual voice messages to each team member every morning rather than relying entirely on group chats, and this small habit has made the relationships feel far more personal.[/44] Sales is fundamentally about trust, so maintaining human connection even at a distance is something I take seriously. The work itself has been largely unaffected, but [50]my company has had to abandon its old model of measuring productivity by hours at a desk and instead focus on whether targets are being met.[/50] That shift has been uncomfortable for some senior managers but I believe it is overdue.
texts.C:
### Rachel, 26, Journalist
I have been working remotely for two years now and one of the most significant changes has been personal. [46]Because I was no longer tied to a particular city by my office, I moved back to my hometown, which has allowed me to be closer to family and dramatically reduced my cost of living.[/46] As a journalist I can work from anywhere with a reliable connection, and that flexibility has genuinely transformed my life. However, [49]what people rarely mention is how isolating it can be when you are new to a career - without an office environment, you miss out on the informal learning that happens simply by observing more experienced colleagues.[/49] That aspect is genuinely difficult to replicate.
texts.D:
### Gordon, 55, University Lecturer
[45]The hardest thing about working from home has been drawing a clear boundary between my working day and the rest of my life - I find myself checking emails at ten in the evening and I know I am not alone in this.[/45] Lectures are now delivered online or in hybrid formats, which suits some students but not others. [47]When colleagues complain about reduced productivity in remote settings, I sometimes wonder whether what they really object to is simply not being able to see their staff.[/47] Visibility is not the same as effectiveness. [52]Whatever arrangements organisations adopt going forward, I believe they will need to trust their employees more and measure success by outcomes rather than presence.[/52]

questions.question: mentions an advantage of working from home that came as a surprise?
questions.explanation: Maya describes the unexpected benefit of being able to concentrate for extended periods without interruptions.

questions.question: describes a specific technique to maintain contact with the people they work with?
questions.explanation: Leon sends individual voice messages to each team member every morning to keep relationships personal.

questions.question: struggles to keep their professional and personal time separate?
questions.explanation: Gordon says the hardest thing has been drawing a clear boundary, and admits he checks emails late in the evening.

questions.question: was able to make a major life change because of remote working?
questions.explanation: Rachel moved back to her hometown because she was no longer tied to a particular city by her office.

questions.question: suggests that some people's concerns about home working may mask a different issue?
questions.explanation: Gordon wonders whether colleagues who complain about reduced productivity really object to not being able to see their staff.

questions.question: values remote working because it allows them to be more available for a family commitment?
questions.explanation: Maya says having more control over her schedule means she can attend her daughter's school events without negotiating time off.

questions.question: identifies a disadvantage of remote working that particularly affects people early in their career?
questions.explanation: Rachel notes that without an office environment, new workers miss out on informal learning by observing experienced colleagues.

questions.question: notes that their employer has had to change the way it measures employees' work?
questions.explanation: Leon says his company has abandoned its old model of measuring productivity by hours at a desk, now focusing on whether targets are met.

questions.question: has seen an improvement in their physical health since working from home?
questions.explanation: Maya started walking at lunchtime every day and has noticed a remarkable improvement in her energy levels.

questions.question: believes organisations will need to fundamentally rethink how they manage people?
questions.explanation: Gordon believes organisations will need to trust employees more and measure success by outcomes rather than presence.
